entry.title: 152 Chrome Live Wallpaper Extensions Hid Ad Tracking and Faked Google Search Traffic
entry.content:
This digest covers a browser-extension monetization cluster reported by Socket’s Threat Research Team involving 152 Chrome Web Store live wallpaper and new-tab extensions. The activity centers on deceptive data disclosures, traffic attribution manipulation, and ad-monetized redirect behavior rather than traditional credential theft, malware delivery, or page-level ad injection. The reporting is notable for the scale of the shared extension ecosystem: approximately 105,000 reported installs across 38 publisher accounts. While the extensions presented themselves as benign customization tools, Socket found inconsistencies between Chrome Web Store data-use claims and linked privacy policy language describing collection of IP addresses, ISP information, click counts, referrers, and data sharing with advertising partners. Socket identified 152 Chrome live wallpaper extensions built from a shared codebase and distributed through multiple publisher accounts. All resolved live listings reportedly claimed in the Chrome Web Store that they did not collect or use user data, while the associated privacy policies stated that telemetry such as IP address, ISP, click counts, and referrer data may be logged and shared with ad partners. A subset of 54 listings tied to tabplugins.com allegedly forged Google organic-search attribution during installation. Socket reported that these extensions used a google.com/url uninstall redirect to make extension-driven traffic appear as though it originated from Google Search, obscuring the true source of visits and potentially improving the perceived legitimacy or value of the traffic. The extensions monetized users primarily by forcing or funneling them toward ad-monetized brand pages, including tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com. The article emphasizes that the behavior did not involve injecting advertisements into pages users browsed; instead, the monetization model depended on controlling new-tab or extension-driven navigation flows. The campaign highlights a recurring browser-extension risk pattern: low-friction installation of consumer-facing customization utilities paired with opaque or contradictory privacy representations. In this case, the Chrome Web Store listings and linked privacy policies reportedly described user-data handling differently, creating a mismatch between public store assurances and external policy disclosures. Socket also observed that every analyzed extension shipped a service-worker routine designed to enumerate and delete IndexedDB entries. The report notes that this routine appeared inert in the analyzed build because the extensions did not store their own state in IndexedDB, but its consistent presence across the shared codebase was still part of the technical footprint connecting the listings. Overall, the reporting describes an ecosystem of Chrome extensions optimized for traffic acquisition, attribution laundering, and advertising monetization. For security teams tracking browser-extension abuse, the cluster illustrates how seemingly benign new-tab experiences can be used to shape user navigation, collect browsing-adjacent telemetry, and generate monetized traffic at scale.

152 Chrome Live Wallpaper Extensions Hid Ad Tracking and Faked Google Search Traffic — Should Read

Socket researchers found 152 Chrome “live wallpaper” extensions that falsely claimed not to collect data while logging user/traffic information and funneling installs to ad-monetized sites. A subset also forged Google organic-search attribution and disguised uninstall pings as Google search-result clicks, turning extension activity into fake organic traffic.

  • Socket's Threat Research Team identified 152 Chrome Web Store new-tab live wallpaper extensions built from a shared codebase and distributed across 38 publisher accounts, with approximately 105,000 reported installs.
  • All resolved live listings claimed in the Chrome Web Store that they would not collect or use user data, while their linked privacy policies stated that the extensions log IP addresses, ISP, click counts, referrers, and share data with ad partners.
  • A 54-listing tabplugins.com subset forged Google organic-search attribution on install and used a google.com/url uninstall redirect to disguise extension-driven traffic as Google search activity.
  • Every analyzed extension shipped an IndexedDB enumerate-and-delete routine in its service worker, but the article states it is inert in this build because the extensions do not store their own state in IndexedDB.
  • The extensions monetized traffic by forcing or funneling users to ad-monetized brand pages on tabplugins.com, yowgames.com, and chromewallpaper.com rather than injecting ads into pages users browse.
entry.summary: This digest covers a browser-extension monetization cluster reported by Socket’s Threat Research Team involving 152 Chrome Web Store live wallpaper and new-tab extensions. The activity centers on deceptive data disclosures, traffic attribution manipulation, and ad-monetized redirect behavior rather...

entry.title: Shai-Hulud, CVE-2026-20253, AUR Hijacks, and Smishing Kits Drive Threat Coverage
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing operators continue scaling smishing operations with real-time data capture and AI-assisted tooling. Zscaler described continued evolution of the Shai-Hulud campaign, which has expanded from npm into PyPI and shifted from maintainer compromise toward CI/CD abuse. The March 2026 Miasma wave reportedly compromised Aqua Security's Trivy vulnerability scanner via GitHub Actions cache poisoning and used Python .pth files for execution at interpreter startup. The May 2026 Hades wave exploited a pullrequesttarget misconfiguration in the TanStack monorepo, scraped OIDC tokens from runner memory, and published malicious npm packages with valid SLSA provenance attestations. The campaign also introduced an AI-focused evasion technique in a June 8 Hades PyPI wave, embedding prompt injection inside an index.js bundle to influence LLM-based security scanners. ThreatLabz assesses earlier waves are linked to TeamPCP, tracked by Mandiant as UNC6780, but attribution is less certain after public release of the worm source code on May 12, 2026. A separate package ecosystem incident affected the Arch Linux AUR, where attackers hijacked more than 400 packages and modified PKGBUILD or .install scripts to run malicious npm or bun install commands during builds. Reported payloads included a Rust credential stealer distributed through atomic-lockfile@1.4.2 and a later bun-based chain using js-digest, with collection focused on browser data, developer tokens, SSH keys, shell histories, container credentials, VPN profiles, and messaging application session data. The malware also reportedly supported an optional eBPF rootkit when executed with sufficient privileges. watchTowr Labs analyzed CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution issue in Splunk Enterprise 10 and later involving the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service Endpoint. The researchers reported that Splunk Enterprise on AWS ships with the sidecar installed and enabled by default, and that Splunk's web application could proxy unauthenticated requests to localhost service APIs such as /en-US/splunkd/raw/v1/postgres/recovery/backup. The reported exploit chain abused backup and restore endpoints, path traversal in the backupFile parameter, PostgreSQL connection-string behavior, and Splunk's .pgpass file to obtain controlled arbitrary file write and code execution as the splunk user. Separately, phpBB fixed a decade-old authentication bypass affecting versions 3.3.16 and below and 4.0.0-a2. Aikido reported that the flaw is exploitable in default configurations with a single HTTP request and can allow login as any user, including administrators; phpBB addressed it in version 3.3.17, while no safe 4.x release was available at publication. Censys detailed a USPS-themed smishing campaign that used a fake Cloudflare security check before serving USPS production assets from phishing infrastructure. The kit opened a WebSocket to its origin and streamed victim card data keystroke by keystroke, while performing server-side BIN lookups and returning routing decisions to the browser. Passive DNS pivots expanded one lure hostname into 682 lookalike hostnames across seven confirmed IPs as of a May 20, 2026 snapshot, with related UPS-themed infrastructure identified through banner-hash pivots. Google said it is suing a Chinese cybercrime network accused of operating the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit and using Gemini AI to generate fraudulent phishing pages supporting SMS phishing attacks against Americans. Google attributed more than 9,000 fake websites and over 1.59 million fraudulent URLs to Outsider between November 14, 2025, and April 14, 2026, and said the service offers more than 290 templates, real-time keystroke logging, and campaign tracking dashboards. A related trust-abuse incident affected Maine's public data breach notification portal, which was temporarily disabled after fraudulent breach disclosures impersonating Discord and VRChat were submitted and automatically published. The Maine Attorney General's Office said the false reports were removed and that it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company. Sygnia reported that the China-nexus group Velvet Ant backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components controlling Linux logins, with earliest traces dating back to 2016. The modified authentication software allowed secret-password access, credential capture, and command logging, enabling persistence even after normal containment steps such as password resets and session termination. The targeted network reportedly lacked direct internet access, so the attackers used internet-facing systems, including a web server, as bridges into the isolated environment. The case underscores how compromise of core login components can convert authentication infrastructure itself into the persistence mechanism, complicating incident scoping and recovery.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing...

entry.title: Arch AUR Malware, Coupang Fine, 23andMe Settlement, and Conti Guilty Plea
entry.content: This digest is dominated by software supply-chain risk, large-scale data breach fallout, and legal accountability tied to major cyber incidents. The most technically urgent item is the compromise of hundreds of Arch User Repository packages distributing Linux malware, while the largest institutional impacts involve record regulatory penalties, breach settlements, and a ransomware prosecution. More than 400 Arch User Repository packages were reported compromised to distribute a Linux rootkit and infostealer through malicious install scripts. The packages invoked npm to download and execute a malicious package named atomic-lockfile, with at least one sample containing a Linux ELF payload described as a credential stealer with optional root-only eBPF rootkit capabilities. Sonatype reported that the attacker hijacked at least 20 orphaned AUR packages and altered their PKGBUILD files to execute the malicious dependency during installation. A separate report from Flare focused on early warning signs of software supply-chain compromise visible in underground forums and marketplaces. The research highlighted listings for GitHub access, private repositories, source code, API keys, OAuth tokens, cloud credentials, CI/CD data, and package registry access as indicators that may precede public incident disclosure. The article tied these patterns to incidents involving Vercel, Sportradar, Mistral AI, Shai-Hulud, LiteLLM, and malicious VS Code extensions, emphasizing how developer environments and trusted software channels can become attack paths. Together, these reports reinforce a recurring theme: attackers continue to target the trust relationships that underpin modern software delivery. Package maintainers, orphaned projects, developer credentials, CI/CD workflows, and repository access remain high-value assets because compromise at these points can propagate malware or expose downstream organizations. South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission imposed a record 624.7 billion won, or approximately $409 million, fine on Coupang and Coupang Fulfillment Services after a breach affecting tens of millions of users. Investigators found that 33,222,472 registered members and at least 4,338,368 non-members were affected, with exposed non-member data including names, phone numbers, and addresses stored as delivery recipient information. The regulator said a former employee used a stolen signing key tied to an alternative authentication system to access data over seven months without detection. The Coupang investigation also included a criminal referral over alleged destruction of evidence after the company manually deleted about six months of web access logs and allowed other records to be automatically wiped. Regulators further found that Coupang covertly collected third-party browsing activity from about 11.2 million users through its Coupang Partners affiliate marketing program without consent. In the United States, a Missouri bankruptcy court administrator approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of the 2023 23andMe data breach. About 7 million customers had data stolen beginning in April 2023, with portions later posted on the dark web. Nearly 256,000 claims have been resolved, with victims eligible for payments ranging from $50 to up to $10,000 depending on claim severity. Ukrainian national Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko pleaded guilty in the United States to conspiracy to commit wire fraud for his role in Conti ransomware activity conducted between 2021 and 2022. Prosecutors said he and co-conspirators deployed Conti ransomware against victims in the United States and abroad, stole data, encrypted systems, and extorted ransom payments in Bitcoin. Court documents state that the Conti operation targeted more than 1,000 victims worldwide and collected over $150 million. The case adds to the continuing legal aftermath of the Conti ransomware ecosystem, which became one of the most prominent ransomware operations of its era. Lytvynenko admitted to joining the conspiracy around September 2021, possessing stolen data from eight U.S. victims and four overseas victims, and working on a malware loader for the operation. He was extradited from Ireland after a July 2023 arrest and faces a maximum sentence of 20 years in prison. Separately, Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act was poised to lapse after congressional renewal efforts stalled. A March intelligence court approval may allow existing orders to continue for another year, but the government likely cannot seek new orders if the authority expires. A central unresolved issue is whether major U.S. communications providers will continue complying with Section 702 court orders after expiration. Jamf announced that the JNUC 2026 session catalog is live, with programming centered on the Apple platform for enterprise AI. The agenda includes Mac workflows, mobility workflows, compliance, reporting and risk reduction, integrations and APIs, end-user experience, and community-focused sessions. The event is scheduled for September 23–25, 2026, in Kansas City.
entry.summary: This digest is dominated by software supply-chain risk, large-scale data breach fallout, and legal accountability tied to major cyber incidents. The most technically urgent item is the compromise of hundreds of Arch User Repository packages distributing Linux malware, while the largest...

entry.title: Agentjacking, CompleteFTP Weak Keys, and Novo Nordisk Clinical Trial Breach
entry.content: This digest window was dominated by risks emerging at trusted integration points: AI coding agents consuming external diagnostics, SaaS and CRM workflows carrying attacker-controlled instructions, and enterprise software producing weak cryptographic material. Reporting also highlighted operational security concerns around clinical trial data exposure and Windows update deployment failures in managed environments. Tenet Security researchers described “Agentjacking,” a class of attack in which AI coding agents can be induced to execute arbitrary code on developer machines. The reported chain abuses a public Sentry DSN to submit a malicious error event containing crafted markdown; when a developer asks tools such as Claude Code or Cursor to resolve Sentry issues through an MCP server, the agent may interpret attacker-supplied content as trusted diagnostic guidance and run code with the developer’s privileges. The researchers said they identified at least 2,388 organizations with valid injectable DSNs and observed an 85% exploitation success rate in controlled testing across widely used AI coding assistants. The finding reflects a broader theme in this window: AI-enabled workflows are increasingly operating across sensitive trust boundaries, inheriting user permissions, and acting autonomously on data from external systems. That theme also appeared in coverage of AppOmni’s 2026 Intellyx Digital Innovator Award and its SaaS and AI security positioning. The article emphasized that AI-enabled SaaS services expand attack surface because they interact with customer data, inherit permissions, and can operate without posture guardrails; AppOmni highlighted Marlin AI, AskOmni, and AgentGuard as capabilities for SaaS security investigation, GenAI assistance, monitoring, policy management, prompt blocking, and third-party integration control. A separate industry article argued that traditional MDR models struggle with AI-assisted attacker speed and alert volume, citing claims that many alerts go unreviewed and that real threats may originate in low-severity or informational signals. IRONSCALES reported an invoice phishing case in which an attacker used FedEx Freight’s Salesforce CRM to send a rebill request to a logistics and distribution company. The message had no malicious links or attachments, was sent via Salesforce Quick Action, carried a valid DKIM signature for fedexfreight.com, passed DMARC, and returned compauth=100, while a downstream Securence relay introduced an SPF softfail because the relay IP was not in the original sender’s SPF record. The payload was purely textual: a request to change the bill-to entity on freight invoice #0584162950. Themis scored the message at 83% confidence as invoice phishing and four mailboxes were quarantined, illustrating how business email compromise-style activity can leverage legitimate CRM infrastructure and authenticated sender identity rather than conventional malware delivery. Novo Nordisk separately disclosed that attackers accessed internal IT systems and copied non-public clinical trial data. The exposed information included pseudonymized patient IDs, trial participation details, sex, year of birth, biomarkers, health and immunogenicity data, and lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol use, and BMI; the company said the data was not directly linked to patient names or other direct identifiers. Information for healthcare professionals was also exposed, including names, registration numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, WhatsApp details, and office locations, while Novo Nordisk said compromised systems were taken offline and core business operations were not impacted. Trail of Bits reported a polynomial-based cryptanalytic technique for factoring RSA moduli with structured, regularly spaced zero-bit patterns. Researchers found hundreds of unique RSA public keys in real-world datasets whose corresponding private keys could be recovered quickly, including keys generated by vulnerable CompleteFTP versions affected by a big-integer type mismatch in which 8-bit random values were copied into 32-bit limbs during key generation. CompleteFTP versions 10.0.0 through 12.0.0 generated vulnerable RSA keys, while versions 10.0.0 through 23.0.4 generated vulnerable DSA keys. The researchers recovered private keys for 603 unique RSA public keys and 74 DSA keys generated by affected versions, and EnterpriseDT released updated detection and checking tools. Microsoft fixed a known issue affecting Windows updates released since May 2025 when installed using the Windows Update Standalone Installer from a network share. The problem affected Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025 enterprise systems, producing ERRORBADPATHNAME in scenarios involving WUSA or double-clicked .msu files from network shares containing multiple .msu files; Microsoft addressed it in June 2026 cumulative updates KB5079391 for Windows 11 and KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025.
entry.summary: This digest window was dominated by risks emerging at trusted integration points: AI coding agents consuming external diagnostics, SaaS and CRM workflows carrying attacker-controlled instructions, and enterprise software producing weak cryptographic material. Reporting also highlighted operational...

entry.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, LangGraph RCE Chain, and Cybercrime Platform Takedowns
entry.content: This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government users, underscoring continued risk around collaboration platforms and identity compromise. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, within three days under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The flaw affects Ivanti Sentry, formerly MobileIron Sentry, and was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after confirmed exploitation in attacks. Shadowserver reported that attackers had already backdoored many internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry gateways and warned that unpatched systems are likely compromised. The directive reflects the conditions defined in BOD 26-04: public exposure, KEV listing, potential for automated exploitation at scale, and attacker ability to gain partial or total control of affected systems. Researchers also disclosed three now-patched vulnerabilities in LangGraph, including a critical exploit chain affecting self-hosted deployments. The chain combines CVE-2025-67644, a SQL injection flaw in the SQLite checkpoint implementation affecting langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite versions before 3.0.1, with CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue affecting langgraph versions before 1.0.10. In self-hosted environments using the SQLite checkpointer with user-controlled filter input, the issues can be chained to execute attacker-controlled code; Check Point said LangChain’s managed LangSmith Deployment platform is not affected by the chain. INTERPOL-led Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform between October 2025 and February 2026. Authorities from 13 Middle East and North Africa countries made 201 arrests, including Guedz, identified as the primary developer and administrator, and seized hardware containing phishing software and scripts. Sniper Dz provided ready-made phishing kits, hosting infrastructure, and operational support targeting brands including PayPal, Facebook, Instagram, Yahoo, Netflix, and Steam. Group-IB said the platform had collected more than 45,000 victim records and was associated with more than 20,000 unique domains, reflecting the industrialized scale of phishing enablement services. Europol also reported the disruption of AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs and other cybercriminal networks to launder more than €336 million since 2021. The June 10 operation included arrests of two alleged administrators in Georgia, searches, takedowns of 25 domains, seizure of more than 30 servers, and blocking of Telegram accounts used by the network. U.S. authorities charged Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev with money laundering-related offenses, while Europol said AudiA6 relied on thousands of fraudulent exchange accounts and more than 6,000 KYC records tied to money mule accounts. France’s interministerial digital directorate, DINUM, said a breach of the Tchap encrypted messaging platform may have affected 73,467 public-sector employees, representing less than 9% of registered users. The attacker accessed Tchap using a compromised user account, which DINUM said it identified and immediately blocked. DINUM stated that private Tchap conversations remain encrypted and protected, while public chat rooms are not encrypted and their shared data may have been accessed. Potentially exposed account data includes last name, first name, email address, public-sector entity, and avatar. A threat actor claimed responsibility for the breach and alleged they scraped nearly 650,000 messages, information from more than 73,000 accounts, and documents and media files shared through the platform. The incident highlights the exposure created when compromised user accounts intersect with semi-public collaboration spaces, even where private messaging remains encrypted.
entry.summary: This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government...

entry.title: CVE-2026-50751 Check Point VPN Bypass and Cloud DNS Takeover SEO Campaign
entry.content: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit legitimacy from trusted enterprise environments. Check Point released hotfixes on June 8, 2026 for CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass rated CVSS 9.3 affecting Mobile Access/SSL VPN, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products. watchTowr Labs analyzed the issue and attributed it to deprecated IKEv1 VPN code, where attacker-controlled input in a VPNExtFeatures Vendor ID payload could influence authentication-related flags inside the gateway implementation. According to the research, the vulnerable implementation could be made to skip certificate signature validation during IKE authentication. watchTowr demonstrated that a vulnerable gateway accepted a random HASHI and created a phase-1 IKE security association, while a patched system rejected the same attempt with an authentication failure. The bypass reportedly worked across UDP 500 and TCP 443 Visitor Mode. The article states that an attacker could authenticate as a provisioned Remote Access user by presenting a matching subject distinguished name, a self-signed X.509 certificate, and an invalid signature, because the gateway did not verify the signature or certificate trust chain. watchTowr reported successful exploitation across Certificate, Certificate with enrollment, and Mixed user authentication modes, making the issue relevant to multiple Remote Access deployment configurations. Cyble Research & Intelligence Labs reported an SEO poisoning campaign that served Thai-language gambling content from trusted enterprise domains by exploiting abandoned or misconfigured DNS infrastructure. The campaign affected 163 organizations across more than 30 countries, with the primary technique involving Azure DNS zone takeover through reclaimed nameserver delegations tied to deleted Azure DNS zones. The campaign also used DigitalOcean DNS zone takeovers, direct wildcard DNS misconfigurations, and individually created A records in at least one Verizon subdomain environment. By inheriting authority over abandoned DNS delegations, the operators were able to publish gambling pages under otherwise legitimate organizational domains, increasing the apparent trust and search visibility of the content. Cyble described a consistent technical footprint across the gambling kit, including valid Let’s Encrypt wildcard TLS certificates, a recurring Next.js build fingerprint, Thai-language SEO content, and affiliate redirect codes such as ibiza99vip1, bigwinv1, link99, and seven77vip1. The infrastructure was linked to three OVH delivery nodes and a 103-node backend fleet in Hong Kong under AS398478, with shared indicators including a broker-xm.com certificate and an identical HTTP body hash.
entry.summary: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit...

entry.title: Shai-Hulud, AUR Hijacks, Ivanti Sentry, Splunk CVE-2026-20253, and AI Agent RCE
entry.content: This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, authentication and remote-access vulnerabilities, and phishing ecosystems increasingly abusing trusted platforms and AI-enabled workflows. Reporting also highlights long-running intrusion tradecraft, large-scale data exposure consequences, and law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime infrastructure. The Shai-Hulud campaign continued to evolve beyond its npm origins into PyPI and CI/CD environments, with Zscaler describing Miasma and Hades waves that abused GitHub Actions cache poisoning, Python .pth persistence, pullrequesttarget misconfiguration, OIDC token scraping, and malicious packages published with valid SLSA provenance. The June 8 Hades PyPI wave also embedded prompt injection intended to mislead LLM-based security scanners, while the public release of the worm source code under an MIT license has made later attribution less certain. Arch Linux’s AUR saw a major package compromise affecting more than 400 packages, where modified build scripts pulled malicious npm or Bun-delivered payloads such as atomic-lockfile and js-digest. The malware targeted developer credentials, browser and Electron app sessions, GitHub and npm tokens, Vault tokens, SSH keys, container credentials, shell histories, and VPN profiles, with optional eBPF rootkit functionality when executed with sufficient privileges. Broader supply-chain risk reporting focused on dark web precursors, including advertisements for GitHub access, private repositories, source code, API keys, CI/CD data, cloud credentials, and OAuth tokens. Separately, Trail of Bits and the badkeys project identified hundreds of factorable “short-sleeve” RSA and DSA keys tied in part to flawed key generation in older EnterpriseDT CompleteFTP versions. watchTowr detailed CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution path in Splunk Enterprise 10+ involving the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service. Although the service listens on localhost, researchers showed it could be reached through Splunk web routes and chained backup and restore behavior with PostgreSQL features to achieve controlled file write as the splunk user and overwrite a Splunk Python script. CISA ordered rapid federal remediation of Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw under active exploitation, with Shadowserver reporting widespread exploitation and many already-backdoored exposed gateways. Check Point also released hotfixes for CVE-2026-50751, an IKEv1 authentication bypass in Mobile Access, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products, where crafted VPN extension data could cause certificate signature validation to be skipped. Other exposed software included phpBB, which fixed a decade-old authentication bypass allowing login as any user in default configurations, and LangGraph, where a SQL injection flaw and unsafe msgpack deserialization could be chained to remote code execution in certain self-hosted deployments. Microsoft also resolved Windows update installation failures affecting WUSA use from network shares on Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025. Tenet Security described “Agentjacking,” an attack class in which malicious Sentry error events containing crafted markdown can be retrieved through an MCP server and interpreted by AI coding agents such as Claude Code or Cursor as trusted diagnostic guidance. The attack begins with a public Sentry DSN and can lead to code execution on developer systems when agents act on attacker-controlled issue content. AI and SaaS security themes also appeared in reporting on AppOmni’s award announcement and commentary on MDR evolution, both emphasizing expanding attack surfaces as AI-enabled services inherit permissions, interact with enterprise data, and automate activity. These pieces framed AI as both an operational security dependency and a potential accelerator for attacker and defender workflows. Censys traced a USPS-themed smishing kit from a single hostname to 682 lookalike domains, with fake Cloudflare verification, USPS production assets, live Google Analytics reuse, WebSocket-based keystroke streaming of payment data, server-side BIN lookups, and operator-controlled routing prompts. Google separately sued a Chinese cybercrime network accused of operating the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit and using Gemini AI to support smishing, with Google attributing 9,000 fake sites and more than 1.59 million fraudulent URLs to the service. INTERPOL’s Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform, with 201 arrests across 13 countries and seizure of infrastructure tied to more than 45,000 victim records and over 20,000 domains. In another trust-abuse case, an attacker used FedEx Freight’s Salesforce CRM to send a DKIM-valid, DMARC-passing invoice rebill request with no link or attachment payload. Cyble reported a large SEO poisoning campaign exploiting abandoned cloud DNS delegations, especially Azure DNS zone takeovers, to host Thai-language gambling content under the domain authority of 163 organizations. The campaign used wildcard records, valid Let’s Encrypt certificates, affiliate redirect codes, and geo-filtering to route Thai users to gambling destinations. Sygnia attributed a long-running intrusion to China-linked Velvet Ant, which backdoored Linux PAM and OpenSSH login components to enable secret-password access, credential and command logging, and log-disabling controls. The activity reportedly dated back to 2016 and persisted in a network segment without direct internet access by bridging through internet-facing systems. Several breach developments centered on sensitive personal and organizational data. Novo Nordisk disclosed theft of pseudonymized clinical trial data and healthcare professional contact details, France reported that 73,467 Tchap messenger accounts were affected after access through a compromised account, and South Korea fined Coupang $409 million after a breach affecting tens of millions of customers and recipients. Maine temporarily disabled public access to its breach notification portal after fraudulent disclosures impersonating Discord and VRChat were automatically published. A bankruptcy administrator also approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of the 2023 23andMe breach, while a Ukrainian national pleaded guilty for his role in Conti ransomware operations and Europol disrupted the AudiA6 crypto-laundering service allegedly used to clean more than €336 million since 2021.
entry.summary: This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, authentication and remote-access vulnerabilities, and phishing ecosystems increasingly abusing trusted platforms and AI-enabled workflows. Reporting also highlights long-running intrusion tradecraft, large-scale data exposure...
